name: Stardust and Spotlights
description:
Musical theatre includes many genres within it, such as classical singing, pop/rock, jazz, folk music, and contemporary music, and it has evolved over the years. The programme will include songs from the golden age of musical theatre with classic tunes from The Sound of Music, The King and I, and Carousel to current musical theatre with catchy tunes from Hamilton, Mean Girls, Frozen, and Wicked. Aleyna Mohanraj has sung on the West End in London in various concerts and pantomimes, one of the highest levels of performance in musical theatre. 
Supported by: 
 
           
Access contact information for Furtados here.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Jaaji
description:
‘Help, I’m shrinking!’ 
Jaaji always follows her heart. She is tall, strong, and brave: a woman who knows exactly what her elephants need and does what needs to be done. 
Then she meets Max. And slowly, without noticing, she does whatever Max likes. She becomes small, weak, fearful. She neglects her giants. 
A play in both English and Kannada, her story is told through live music, masks, Muppets, Bunraku-style puppets, and finger puppets. A playful, colourful theatre performance for young audiences and adults alike, Jaaji invites you to celebrate your own shape, size, and courage. 
Credits:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Nirbhay Nirgun
description:
‘Nirbhay Nirgun Gun Re Gaunga,’ Kabir wrote. 
The fearless, nirbhay, and the formless, nirgun: this is the aspiration for the mind. 
Nirbhay Nirgun is a concert celebrating Kabir’s philosophy and his plain-spoken wisdom through song, interspersed with discussion that contextualises the verses within today’s challenging socio-political climate. Hindustani classical musicians Nimish and Avanti bring this journey to life through some of Kabir’s most iconic writing — settings immortalised by Kumar Gandharva and Madhup Mudgal, compositions by their guru Padma Shri Dr Ashwini Bhide Deshpande, and new compositions by Nimish himself. 
Kahat Kabir Suno Bhai Sadho, Jeet Nishan Ghuraunga! 
Credits
Writer- Avanti Patel
Vocals- Avanti Patel & Nimish Kaikadi
Tabla- Vighnesh Kamath
Harmonium- Vishvajith Kini
Percussions- Shamdath Ravi 
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Found Wanting
description:
Open Instagram for ten minutes. Notice what happens to your body. 
You didn’t log on hungry. But now you want a better apartment, a different body, a trip you’d never considered. Nobody forced you. You just saw a few images… 
This is the most underexamined form of influence in modern life: the image that tells you what contentment looks like, what satisfaction looks like. And because it is visual, it bypasses argument entirely. You do not disagree with a picture; you simply feel lacking. Wanting is not a problem, until it gets hijacked: engineered for urgency, designed to never satisfy, optimised to keep you starving. 
This is a small-group workshop for the curious. Using drawing, writing, and mind-mapping, you will learn to slow the machinery down and look at it directly. Where do your wants actually come from? Which ones are yours? 
This workshop has limited capacity. Please register your interest only if you are able to stay for the entire duration of the event. Participants will be selected based on responses to the RSVP form, and will receive an email confirming a spot. 

In collaboration with:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Reimagined Landscapes
description:
Between good intentions and meaningful action, there is often just an overwhelming amount of information and nowhere to put it. 
This session begins with a screening of The Becoming of a Wildscape, followed by a conversation with Abi Vanak. Ecologist and Director of the Centre for Policy Design at the Ashoka Trust for Research in Ecology and the Environment (ATREE), Vanak distils years of field experience into accessible and actionable insights. The session then opens to audience questions. 
Less about how much we stand to lose, and more about what we can actually do, the evening centres on one question: what does restoration look like when it is responsive to landscapes, communities, and climates? 
The Becoming of a Wildscape
18:09 min | India | Directed by Tanmayi Gidh | Created by Untold Earth and Teepoi Films 
The first in an anthology of five short films on ecological restoration by Untold Earth, The Becoming of a Wildscape follows Zerodha’s ecological restoration initiative, Elephants By The Lake. Centring founder Nithin Kamath, CTO Kailash Nadh, and Programme Manager Varun Balakrishnan, the film explores their thinking and approach, guided by curiosity, experimentation, and a genuine willingness to learn. The film hopes to offer a blueprint of what it can look like for businesses, and the individuals who run them to care for ecosystems and communities. 

Image Credits: Teepoi Films 
In collaboration with:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Yugant
description:
A decade ago, the Deshpande family had a home. Now all that remains is a ruin. 
The walls are torn and the sun is unrelenting. Vultures circle. Water runs scarce. The once-formidable stone mansion of Dharangaon has become a shadow of itself, just as the family that built it. Some have died, others have left. Only three remain.  
When cousin Abhay returns from the cold of Sweden, he only finds dust. Four people in a dying landscape, asking the questions that comfort and distance had kept at bay: What do we owe the places that made us? What do we leave behind for our children? And when everything has already been lost, what is there left to mourn? 
Yugant, performed in Hindi, is the final part of Mahesh Elkunchwar’s celebrated Wada Chirebandi trilogy: the culmination of a family saga that has tracked one household across decades of quiet unravelling.  
Supported by:

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Knockers
description:
Across the country, volunteers are knitting and crocheting prosthetic breasts for women who’ve had mastectomies. Many of them are survivors themselves. Through the act of creation, they restore something that surgery cannot: dignity, agency, a sense of self. Knockers carries the same spirit. 
Intimate. Unapologetic. Defiant. 
This one-person performance is built from five monologues. A model who built her life around her body. A teacher who meets her diagnosis with Shakespeare. A survivor preparing for her first intimate encounter after surgery. A non-binary individual navigating a medical system that can’t see them. An immigrant carrying cultural silence in two languages. Together, their stories weave a tapestry of grief, rage, humour, resistance, and radical self-definition. 
When the body changes, who decides what remains sacred?

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Phantoms of the Past
description:
Delhi is deeply haunted. What it is haunted by, is a different question altogether.  
In the shadow of medieval mosques and Mughal tombs, the past refuses to stay buried. Saints, Sultans, poets, and lovers—all linger in the city’s imagination, their stories shaping how we remember what once was. Ghosted is Eric Chopra’s account of why folklore clusters around ruins, why humans are drawn to places with reputations for the uncanny, and why history, everywhere in the world, is so haunted.  
The evening begins with a dramatic reading from Ghosted, followed by a conversation between Eric Chopra and Vikramajit Ram, with visuals of the monuments. The session then opens to an audience Q&A.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India

name: Rights, Reality, Red Flags
description:
India has a brilliant constitution with nuanced laws on preventive detention, manual scavenging, military justice, free speech. Reality, as we well know, isn’t always so perfectly packaged. 
Discovery of New India (Conditions Apply) is a graphic novel by Aakar Patel and political cartoonist PenPencilDraw that moves through courtrooms, prisons, police stations, and the quiet machinery of bureaucracy to show how rights in contemporary India often exist with conditions attached. What makes it unusual is its form: stark, unsparing illustration that makes legal and constitutional realities, the kind that would otherwise stay buried in fine print, feel visceral and immediate. This is reportage you can’t look away from. 
Aakar Patel joins journalist Priya Ramani for a conversation about how the book was made: the rigorous research, the visual language it demanded, and the particular courage it takes to illustrate how power actually behaves. The session then opens to audience questions.

location.name: Bangalore International Centre, 7, 4th Main Road, Domlur II Stage, Bangalore, Karnataka, 560071, India
